# Session 3 extrinsic risk factors

This document discusses extrinsic risk factors that can lead to sports injuries. It defines extrinsic risks as external factors like equipment, other players, coaching, technique, and environment conditions. The document explains that identifying these extrinsic risks is important for understanding how injuries can be prevented. It prompts students to consider how risks in their chosen sport could be reduced to minimize potential injuries from external factors.

# 1 Causes of sport injury

Sport injuries can be caused by a wide variety of factors. These factors can be grouped into four main areas: physical, environmental, socio-cultural and psychological (Wiese-Bjornstal and Shaffer, 1999) as illustrated in Figure 1.

![Causes of sport injury](https://www.open.edu/openlearn/pluginfile.php/1427344/mod_oucontent/oucontent/75636/07ed9a2f/157e2c14/e235_2_ol_s3_sec1_fig2.tif.jpg)

Physical and environmental factors are perhaps more obviously associated with injury, but the links between socio-cultural and psychological factors and injury are less obvious. Socio-cultural factors relate to the culture and attitudes that are often adopted within sport that could encourage the development of a sport injury. Some examples of the attitudes that could increase the risk of injury include: the belief that pain tolerance demonstrates strength and toughness, an acceptance that pain and injury are part of sport (‘no pain, no gain’), and an unwillingness to seek medical treatment for fear of appearing weak (Wiese-Bjornstal and Shaffer, 1999).

Meeuwise et al. (2007) suggest that the risk factors for developing an injury can be split into intrinsic risk factors and extrinsic risk factors:

Next you’ll look at psychological factors in more detail.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Weather, terrain, and playing surfaces significantly influence injury risk. Adverse conditions like rain or poor field quality can lead to slips and falls.
Using proper, well-fitted equipment reduces injury risk. Poor quality or ill-fitting gear can cause misalignment and increased susceptibility to injuries.
Inadequate coaching techniques and training regimens can lead to overexertion and improper form, contributing to higher injury rates among athletes.
Different sports have unique physical demands that may predispose athletes to specific injuries. Understanding these can aid in tailored injury prevention strategies.
Higher competition levels often increase intensity and pressure, leading to a greater risk of injuries due to fatigue and heightened physical demands.
